questions.prompt: Ask in this order:
Was this an Illinois state case, federal case, or out-of-state case?
Were you under 18 when it happened, and was it handled in juvenile court or adult court?
What county, court, and case number?
Was there an arrest, ticket, or charge not initiated by arrest?

How did the case end: released without charging, dismissed, acquitted, no probable cause, supervision, qualified probation, conviction, reversed/vacated conviction, pardon, certificate of innocence, or still pending?
What was the exact charge and statute?

Was the case a DUI, reckless driving, minor traffic offense, domestic battery, protection-order/no-contact violation, sex offense, offense against a minor, animal cruelty, registry offense, or violent offense?
Did you successfully complete supervision or probation?
Was it qualified probation under a drug/cannabis/meth/second-chance/substance-use statute?
When did the last sentence, supervision, or qualified probation terminate?
Do you have any pending criminal charges?
Was the case cannabis-related? If yes, amount, statute, date, and violent-crime connection?
Was the case connected to trafficking, coercion, exploitation, survival circumstances, or felony prostitution?
Was someone else using your name or identity?
Do you have your ISP Access and Review RAP sheet, court dispositions, and Chicago RAP sheet if applicable?
How old were you when this happened?
Have you finished everything the court ordered?
questions.helperText: jail/probation/parole, classes, community service, anything still owed

pathways.label: Adult non-conviction expungement
pathways.summary: Under 20 ILCS 2630/5.2(b), a person may petition to expunge arrests or charges not initiated by arrest when each record resulted in: Acquittal Eligible immediately unless excluded Dismissal Eligible immediately unless excluded Released without charging Eligible immediately unless excluded Conviction reversed or vacated Eligible immediately unless excluded
ruleClauses: Under 20 ILCS 2630/5.2(b), a person may petition to expunge arrests or charges not initiated by arrest when each record resulted in:
Acquittal Eligible immediately unless excluded
Dismissal Eligible immediately unless excluded
Released without charging Eligible immediately unless excluded
Conviction reversed or vacated Eligible immediately unless excluded
Successful supervision Eligible after waiting period
Successful qualified probation Eligible after waiting period

The statute says expungement is available for acquittal, dismissal, release without charging, reversed/vacated conviction, successful supervision, and successful qualified probation, subject to exclusions. (
"https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")
Adult expungement waiting periods
Expungement category Waiting period
Released without charging No wait
Conviction reversed/vacated No wait
Most successful supervision 2 years after satisfactory termination
Supervision for listed offenses, including certain Vehicle Code and specified criminal-code offenses 5 years after satisfactory termination
Supervision for misdemeanor reckless driving before age 25 with no DUI/reckless-driving conviction Not until petitioner reaches age 25
Successful qualified probation 5 years after satisfactory termination
The no-wait, 2-year, 5-year, age-25, and qualified-probation timing rules are set out in 5.2(b)(2). (

includes probation under the Cannabis Control Act, Controlled Substances Act 410, Methamphetamine Control and Community Protection Act 70, certain Unified Code of Corrections provisions, certain old battery provisions, the Illinois Alcoholism and Other Drug Dependency Act, the Substance Use Disorder Act, and the Steroid Control Act. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")

when they mean ordinary probation. Ordinary conviction probation is usually not expungement. Wilma must ask whether it was qualified probation under one of the listed statutes and whether it was successfully completed.
The two remedies are not interchangeable, and a record is usually eligible for one or the other, not both. The
agent's first job is to route the user to the correct remedy.
Expungement (record destroyed or returned)
Available, generally, when the case did NOT end in a conviction:
Arrests or charges that did not lead to a conviction (no charges filed, dismissed, nolle prosequi, stricken with leave, or a finding of not guilty / acquittal).
Cases that ended in court supervision — supervision is not a conviction, so most supervision dispositions are expungeable after the waiting period.
Qualified probation successfully completed and the case dismissed: 410 (drug), 710, 1410, TASC, Second Chance probation.
A conviction that was reversed, vacated, or for which the petitioner was found factually innocent.

Illinois Legal Aids May 2026 Clean Slate FAQ says the June 1, 2026 changes include no drug test requirement, a 2-year wait for successful supervision/qualified probation/conditional discharge/probation completed without revocation/misdemeanor convictions/municipal violations, a 3-year wait for felony cases not in that group, and no waiver of the 2-year waiting period. pathways.label: Cannabis-specific automatic or petition expungement
pathways.summary: Illinois has a special cannabis framework. Minor cannabis offense definition means a violation of Cannabis Control Act 4 or 5 involving not more than 30 grams of cannabis, with no penalty enhancement under 7 and no association with an arrest, conviction, or other disposition for a violent crime. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm") Non-conviction minor cannabis records Illinois State Police says eligible non-conviction minor cannabis arrest records must be automatically expunged on a phased schedule: records from Jan. 1, 2013 to June 25, 2019 by Jan. 1, 2021; records from Jan. 1, 2000 to Jan. 1,…
ruleClauses: Illinois has a special cannabis framework.
Minor cannabis offense definition

means a violation of Cannabis Control Act 4 or 5 involving not more than 30 grams of cannabis, with no penalty enhancement under 7 and no association with an arrest, conviction, or other disposition for a violent crime. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")
Non-conviction minor cannabis records

Illinois State Police says eligible non-conviction minor cannabis arrest records must be automatically expunged on a phased schedule: records from Jan. 1, 2013 to June 25, 2019 by Jan. 1, 2021; records from Jan. 1, 2000 to Jan. 1, 2013 by Jan. 1, 2023; and records before Jan. 1, 2000 by Jan. 1, 2025. ISP also warns that it does not direct or control other law-enforcement agencies compliance, so users should verify with the arresting agency. ("https://isp.illinois.gov/BureauOfIdentification/CannabisExpungements")

ISP identifies eligible minor cannabis conviction records and forwards them to the Prisoner Review Board for the Governors pardon-and-expungement process. If a pardon authorizing expungement is granted, the PRB through the Attorney General files a petition in the circuit where the conviction occurred. Individuals may also file a motion to vacate and expunge certain misdemeanor or Class 4 felony cannabis convictions under 5.2(i)(3). ("https://isp.illinois.gov/BureauOfIdentification/CannabisExpungements")
1 Was it possession, manufacture/delivery, or something else?
2 Was the amount 30 grams or less?
3 Was there a penalty enhancement?
4 Was there a violent-crime arrest/conviction/disposition tied to the same event?
5 Was it a non-conviction, conviction, supervision, or qualified probation?
6 Was it already automatically expunged?
7 Does the user need an ISP Access and Review to verify?
Under the Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act, a "minor cannabis offense" (possession of not more than 30
grams, no penalty enhancement, not tied to a violent crime) committed before June 25, 2019 is eligible for
automatic expungement of the arrest record by the Illinois State Police on a rolling timeline (records pre-2000
by Jan 1 2025; 2000–2012 by Jan 1 2023; 2013–6/25/2019 by Jan 1 2021). Minor-cannabis convictions route
The two remedies are not interchangeable, and a record is usually eligible for one or the other, not both. The
agent's first job is to route the user to the correct remedy.
Expungement (record destroyed or returned)
Available, generally, when the case did NOT end in a conviction:
Arrests or charges that did not lead to a conviction (no charges filed, dismissed, nolle prosequi, stricken with leave, or a finding of not guilty / acquittal).
Cases that ended in court supervision — supervision is not a conviction, so most supervision dispositions are expungeable after the waiting period.
Qualified probation successfully completed and the case dismissed: 410 (drug), 710, 1410, TASC, Second Chance probation.
A conviction that was reversed, vacated, or for which the petitioner was found factually innocent.
A conviction pardoned by the Governor with express authorization to expunge.
Certain minor cannabis offenses (often handled automatically — see Section 9 and the cannabis note below).
Sealing (record hidden from most public view)

Illinois allows a trafficking victim to petition for vacation and expungement or immediate sealing after completing the last sentence if participation in the underlying offense resulted from human trafficking or a severe form of trafficking under federal law. The petition must state that the person was a trafficking victim at the time of the offense and that their participation resulted from trafficking; eligibility is shown by a preponderance of the evidence. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")

If a juvenile record is not automatically expunged, a person may petition at no cost. For delinquency adjudications not automatically expunged, the person may petition after 2 years have passed since all juvenile court proceedings and any Department of Juvenile Justice commitment terminated, except records based on first-degree murder or Article 11 offenses requiring sex-offender registration at the time of petition. ("https://www.ilga.gov/legislation/ilcs/fulltext?DocName=070504050K5-915&utm_source=chatgpt.com")

Automatic sealing will generally cover records already eligible for sealing once the system begins, including non-convictions, completed supervision/probation, most misdemeanors, and most nonviolent felonies, but the person must have completed the sentence, have no pending criminal charges, and satisfy the waiting period. ("https://www.illinoislegalaid.org/legal-information/what-you-need-know-about-clean-slate-act")

Unlike Mississippi, Illinois HAS a mandatory statewide form suite.

The Illinois Supreme Court Commission on Access to Justice, with the Office of the State Appellate Defender (OSAD), publishes standardized expungement/sealing forms that every Illinois circuit court is required to accept.
The agent should always steer users to these official forms rather than drafting a bespoke pleading.
The core statewide forms 1 Request to Expunge and Impound and/or Seal Criminal Records — the main petition; one form covers both remedies via checkboxes, listing each arrest/case to be cleared.
2 Notice of Filing — served on the State's Attorney, arresting agency, chief legal officer, and Illinois State Police to notify them of the request and start their objection window.
3 Order to Expunge and Impound and/or Seal Criminal Records — the proposed order the judge signs; arrest information must be completed and submitted with the Request.

Where to get them • Illinois Courts Standardized State Forms website (illinoiscourts.gov) — official fillable PDFs. • Office of the State Appellate Defender (osad.illinois.gov) — forms plus eligibility worksheets; OSAD Expungement Unit help line. • Illinois Legal Aid Online (illinoislegalaid.org) — a free guided "Easy Form" interview that assembles the completed forms (it does not e-file them).

Supporting material • The petitioner's statewide criminal-history transcript (RAP sheet) from the Illinois State Police (see Section 9). • Case/disposition information for every arrest and charge being cleared (dates, arresting agency, case number, disposition). • For qualified-probation expungements filed before 6/30/2026, historically a clean drug test within 30 days — the Clean Slate Act removes this requirement effective 6/30/2026.
AGENT GUIDANCE Because the forms are mandatory statewide, never tell a user a county requires a different bespoke petition.
The Request, Notice, and Order travel together; an Order with arrest info must accompany the Request at filing.
Recommend the Illinois Legal Aid Easy Form to users who want guided drafting, but remind them it does not file for them — they still file with the circuit clerk and (often) e-file.

("https://www.illinoislegalaid.org/legal-information/criminal-record-expungement-or-sealing-request")

The circuit clerk serves the petition on the States Attorney/prosecutor, Illinois State Police, arresting agency, and chief legal officer of the local government that made the arrest. The master question is not "misdemeanor or felony" but expungement vs. sealing.
Expungement physically destroys or returns the record and is generally available only for non-convictions and supervision/qualified-probation dispositions.
Sealing hides the record from most public view (but not law enforcement) and is available for most misdemeanor and felony convictions, subject to an exclusion list.
The agent should always (1) determine the disposition type first, (2) decide expungement vs. sealing from that, (3) check the offense against the exclusion list, then (4) apply the waiting period.

sourceSections.title: How Wilma should route Illinois cases
sourceSections.text:
Case outcome / record type First Wilma route Illinois treatment
Released without charging Expungement or sealing Expunge available immediately unless excluded
Dismissal Expungement or sealing Expunge available immediately unless excluded
Acquittal / not guilty Expungement or sealing Expunge available immediately unless excluded
Finding of no probable cause Expungement/sealing analysis Usually treated as non-conviction route
Conviction vacated or reversed Expungement or sealing Expunge available immediately unless excluded
Order of supervision successfully completed Expungement or sealing Expungement wait usually 2 years, sometimes 5 years or age 25
Qualified probation successfully completed Expungement or sealing Expungement wait usually 5 years after successful termination
Misdemeanor conviction Sealing Often sealable unless excluded
Felony conviction Sealing Broadly sealable unless excluded; some serious felonies petition-only, not automatic
Pardon authorizing expungement Expungement Petition to expunge after governors pardon authorizes it
Certificate of innocence Expungement Court granting certificate also enters expungement order
Minor cannabis offense Automatic/pardon/court cannabis expungement Special cannabis routes
Human trafficking-related offense Vacation + expungement or immediate sealing Survivor route under 5.2(h)
Prior Class 4 felony prostitution conviction Automatic sealing and/or motion to vacate and expunge Special 5.2(j) route
Juvenile record Juvenile expungement Separate automatic and petition routes under 705 ILCS 405/5-915
Federal or out-of-state case Not Illinois court relief Illinois courts cannot expunge/seal federal or out-of-state records
Illinois Legal Aid confirms that Illinois courts cannot expunge or seal records from other states or federal cases, even if the federal case happened in Illinois. (

"https://www.illinoislegalaid.org/legal-information/expunging-or-sealing-criminal-record-common-questions")

sourceSections.title: Remedy definitions Wilma must understand
sourceSections.text:
Expungement
For ordinary expungement, the arresting agency, Illinois State Police, and other ordered agencies must expunge the records within statutory timelines. For some expungement categories, court clerk records are impounded rather than physically destroyed, and authorized agencies may still have limited access depending on the route. For public inquiries, the court, Illinois State Police, or agency generally responds as if no record ever existed. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")
Wilma language:
Expungement is the strongest Illinois record-clearing remedy, but some court/state-police records may be impounded or retained for limited authorized uses depending on the route.

Sealing
Illinois defines sealing as physically/electronically maintaining the records, but making them unavailable without a court order, subject to statutory exceptions. The petitioners name is also obliterated from the official circuit clerk index, but earlier-issued indexes are not affected. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")
Wilma language:
Sealing hides the record from most public background checks, but law enforcement, courts, and some authorized employers/licensing agencies may still access it.

sourceSections.title: Official forms Wilma should know
sourceSections.text:
Illinois has approved statewide expungement and sealing forms that all Illinois courts must accept. The current adult statewide packet includes:

Purpose Official statewide form
Instructions How to Clear Your Criminal Record: Expungement & Sealing
Main petition Request to Expunge and/or Seal Criminal Records
Case list Case List for Request to Expunge and/or Seal Criminal Records
Extra expungement cases Additional Arrests or Cases for Expungement
Extra sealing cases Additional Arrests or Cases for Sealing
Proposed/grant order Order to Expunge and/or Seal Criminal Records
Denial order Order Denying Request to Expunge and/or Seal Criminal Records
Clerk service certificate Certificate of Service by Circuit Clerk
The Illinois Courts forms page says the Supreme Court Commission on Access to Justice approved these forms and that all Illinois courts must accept them; the listed adult forms are approved 06/2026. (

"https://www.illinoiscourts.gov/documents-and-forms/approved-forms/circuit-court-standardized-forms-suites/expungement-sealing/") The Office of the State Appellate Defender also states that these adult sealing/expungement forms are legally correct, user-friendly, and required to be accepted in all Illinois courts. ("https://osad.illinois.gov/expungement/expungement-and-sealing-general-information.html")

sourceSections.title: Adult expungement eligibility
sourceSections.text:
Under 20 ILCS 2630/5.2(b), a person may petition to expunge arrests or charges not initiated by arrest when each record resulted in:

Outcome Expungement rule
Acquittal Eligible immediately unless excluded
Dismissal Eligible immediately unless excluded
Released without charging Eligible immediately unless excluded
Conviction reversed or vacated Eligible immediately unless excluded
Successful supervision Eligible after waiting period
Successful qualified probation Eligible after waiting period
The statute says expungement is available for acquittal, dismissal, release without charging, reversed/vacated conviction, successful supervision, and successful qualified probation, subject to exclusions. (

"https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")
Adult expungement waiting periods

Expungement category Waiting period
Released without charging No wait
Dismissal No wait
Acquittal No wait
Conviction reversed/vacated No wait
Most successful supervision 2 years after satisfactory termination
Supervision for listed offenses, including certain Vehicle Code and specified criminal-code offenses 5 years after satisfactory termination
Supervision for misdemeanor reckless driving before age 25 with no DUI/reckless-driving conviction Not until petitioner reaches age 25
Successful qualified probation 5 years after satisfactory termination
The no-wait, 2-year, 5-year, age-25, and qualified-probation timing rules are set out in 5.2(b)(2). (

"https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")
Qualified probation
Illinois
qualified probation
includes probation under the Cannabis Control Act, Controlled Substances Act 410, Methamphetamine Control and Community Protection Act 70, certain Unified Code of Corrections provisions, certain old battery provisions, the Illinois Alcoholism and Other Drug Dependency Act, the Substance Use Disorder Act, and the Steroid Control Act. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")
Wilma caution: Users often say
probation
when they mean ordinary probation. Ordinary conviction probation is usually not expungement. Wilma must ask whether it was qualified probation under one of the listed statutes and whether it was successfully completed.

sourceSections.title: Adult sealing eligibility
sourceSections.text:
Illinois sealing is broader than expungement. Under 20 ILCS 2630/5.2(c), the following records may be sealed unless excluded:

Record type Sealing route
Arrest resulting in release without charging Seal at any time
Acquittal, dismissal, reversed/vacated conviction Seal at any time
Successfully completed supervision Seal after waiting period
Conviction, including municipal ordinance conviction Seal after waiting period unless excluded
First-offender probation under listed drug/cannabis/meth/second-chance routes Seal after waiting period unless excluded
Felony conviction Seal after waiting period unless excluded
The statute lists these categories as eligible sealing records. (

"https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")
Sealing waiting periods - current post-Clean Slate Act guidance
For petition-based sealing, Wilma should use the updated June 1, 2026 Clean Slate Act changes as the working rule:

Sealing category Waiting period
Non-convictions: dismissed, acquitted, released without charge, reversed/vacated conviction Any time
Successful supervision / qualified probation / conditional discharge / probation completed without revocation 2 years after completing the sentence
Misdemeanor convictions and municipal ordinance violations 2 years after completing the sentence
Felony convictions not in the 2-year group 3 years after completing the last felony sentence
Certain 3-year-wait cases where education/career credential was earned during sentence/MSR Waiting period may be waived only for the 3-year category, not the 2-year category
Illinois Legal Aids May 2026 Clean Slate FAQ says the June 1, 2026 changes include no drug test requirement, a 2-year wait for successful supervision/qualified probation/conditional discharge/probation completed without revocation/misdemeanor convictions/municipal violations, a 3-year wait for felony cases not in that group, and no waiver of the 2-year waiting period. (

"https://www.illinoislegalaid.org/legal-information/what-you-need-know-about-clean-slate-act")
Wilma caution: Older materials and older statutory text may still mention a drug test for certain drug-related sealing/expungement routes. Illinois Legal Aids 2026 update says that starting June 1, 2026, no drug test is required for sealing or expunging any eligible record. ("https://www.illinoislegalaid.org/legal-information/what-you-need-know-about-clean-slate-act")

sourceSections.title: Cannabis-specific expungement
sourceSections.text:
Illinois has a special cannabis framework.
Minor cannabis offense definition
A
Minor Cannabis Offense
means a violation of Cannabis Control Act 4 or 5 involving not more than 30 grams of cannabis, with no penalty enhancement under 7 and no association with an arrest, conviction, or other disposition for a violent crime. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")
Non-conviction minor cannabis records
Illinois State Police says eligible non-conviction minor cannabis arrest records must be automatically expunged on a phased schedule: records from Jan. 1, 2013 to June 25, 2019 by Jan. 1, 2021; records from Jan. 1, 2000 to Jan. 1, 2013 by Jan. 1, 2023; and records before Jan. 1, 2000 by Jan. 1, 2025. ISP also warns that it does not direct or control other law-enforcement agencies compliance, so users should verify with the arresting agency. ("https://isp.illinois.gov/BureauOfIdentification/CannabisExpungements")
Minor cannabis convictions
ISP identifies eligible minor cannabis conviction records and forwards them to the Prisoner Review Board for the Governors pardon-and-expungement process. If a pardon authorizing expungement is granted, the PRB through the Attorney General files a petition in the circuit where the conviction occurred. Individuals may also file a motion to vacate and expunge certain misdemeanor or Class 4 felony cannabis convictions under 5.2(i)(3). ("https://isp.illinois.gov/BureauOfIdentification/CannabisExpungements")
Wilma cannabis questions:
1 Was it possession, manufacture/delivery, or something else?
2 Was the amount 30 grams or less?
3 Was there a penalty enhancement?
4 Was there a violent-crime arrest/conviction/disposition tied to the same event?
5 Was it a non-conviction, conviction, supervision, or qualified probation?
6 Was it already automatically expunged?
7 Does the user need an ISP Access and Review to verify?

sourceSections.title: Human trafficking survivor route
sourceSections.text:
Illinois allows a trafficking victim to petition for vacation and expungement or immediate sealing after completing the last sentence if participation in the underlying offense resulted from human trafficking or a severe form of trafficking under federal law. The petition must state that the person was a trafficking victim at the time of the offense and that their participation resulted from trafficking; eligibility is shown by a preponderance of the evidence. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")
Wilma trauma rule: Ask only what is needed to route:

Was this case connected to trafficking, coercion, exploitation, survival circumstances, or someone forcing or directing you to do something connected to the charge?

Do not ask for graphic details. Trigger manual/legal review.

sourceSections.title: Criminal identity theft / mistaken identity
sourceSections.text:
If someone was arrested or convicted under the name of a person whose identity was stolen or used without authorization, the aggrieved person may petition the chief judge of the circuit where the arrest occurred to correct the arrest/conviction and official records by removing their name and substituting the offenders name if known. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")
Wilma rule: If the user says
that was not me,

someone used my name,
or
identity theft,
route to the criminal-identity-theft correction process, not ordinary expungement/sealing first.

sourceSections.title: Juvenile expungement
sourceSections.text:
Juvenile records use 705 ILCS 405/5-915, not the adult 20 ILCS 2630/5.2 process.
Automatic juvenile expungement
Illinois law provides automatic juvenile expungement for several categories, including juvenile law-enforcement records for events before age 18 under specified conditions, and juvenile court/law-enforcement records after dismissal of a delinquency petition, finding of not delinquent, successful supervision termination, or successful termination of adjudication for conduct that would be a Class B misdemeanor, Class C misdemeanor, petty offense, or business offense if committed by an adult. ("https://www.ilga.gov/legislation/ilcs/fulltext?DocName=070504050K5-915&utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Petition-based juvenile expungement
If a juvenile record is not automatically expunged, a person may petition at no cost. For delinquency adjudications not automatically expunged, the person may petition after 2 years have passed since all juvenile court proceedings and any Department of Juvenile Justice commitment terminated, except records based on first-degree murder or Article 11 offenses requiring sex-offender registration at the time of petition. ("https://www.ilga.gov/legislation/ilcs/fulltext?DocName=070504050K5-915&utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma juvenile rule: Ask whether the case was before age 18, whether it stayed in juvenile court or was prosecuted as adult, whether there was a delinquency adjudication, whether proceedings/commitment are over, and whether the offense was first-degree murder or an Article 11 sex offense with current registration.

sourceSections.title: Clean Slate automatic sealing - important 2026/2029 note
sourceSections.text:
Illinois Clean Slate Act changes some sealing rules and creates a future automatic sealing system, but it does not create automatic expungement and does not change eligibility for expungement/vacatur. Automatic sealing is expected to begin in 2029, so users should not assume eligible records are sealed now. Illinois Legal Aid says people still need to file a petition if they want sealing or expungement sooner. ("https://www.illinoislegalaid.org/legal-information/what-you-need-know-about-clean-slate-act")
Automatic sealing will generally cover records already eligible for sealing once the system begins, including non-convictions, completed supervision/probation, most misdemeanors, and most nonviolent felonies, but the person must have completed the sentence, have no pending criminal charges, and satisfy the waiting period. ("https://www.illinoislegalaid.org/legal-information/what-you-need-know-about-clean-slate-act")

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
Step 1 - Get records
Wilma should tell users to gather:
Illinois State Police Access and Review / RAP sheet;
certified court dispositions for every case;
Chicago Police RAP sheet if there were Chicago arrests;
case numbers, arrest dates, arresting agencies, and final dispositions.
ISP says individuals may use the Access and Review process by going to an Illinois law-enforcement/correctional facility or licensed fingerprint vendor, submitting fingerprints and identifying information, and receiving their criminal-history transcript; the process also includes a Record Challenge form for correcting inaccurate information. ("https://isp.illinois.gov/BureauOfIdentification/MyRecord?utm_source=chatgpt.com") Illinois Legal Aid says an ISP statewide transcript includes Illinois arrests and convictions and that Chicago arrests may require a Chicago RAP sheet as well. ("https://www.illinoislegalaid.org/legal-information/how-get-copies-your-criminal-records?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Step 2 - Choose route by disposition
Use this order:
1 Juvenile or adult?
2 Federal/out-of-state or Illinois?
3 Non-conviction or conviction?
4 Supervision or qualified probation?
5 Cannabis?
6 Trafficking/prostitution survivor?
7 Identity theft?
8 Pardon/certificate of innocence?
9 Ordinary sealing?
Step 3 - File in the correct county/circuit
The petition is filed with the clerk of the court where the arrests occurred or where charges were brought. If arrests or charges were in multiple jurisdictions, a separate petition must be filed in each jurisdiction. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm") Illinois Legal Aids Easy Form page also warns that if a person wants to expunge or seal records in more than one county, they must complete the program for each county separately; if they have records in more than one Cook County district, they must complete it for each district separately. ("https://www.illinoislegalaid.org/legal-information/criminal-record-expungement-or-sealing-request")
Step 4 - Service and objections
The circuit clerk serves the petition on the States Attorney/prosecutor, Illinois State Police, arresting agency, and chief legal officer of the local government that made the arrest. Objections must be filed within 60 days after service. If an objection is filed, the court sets a hearing. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")
Step 5 - Order and compliance
After an order is entered, the court sends the order to ISP, the petitioner, prosecutor, arresting agency, local chief legal officer, and any other ordered criminal justice agencies. Agencies generally must comply within 60 days after service, subject to motion/appeal rules. ISP must send written notice of compliance when required. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")

sourceSections.title: Fees
sourceSections.text:
Fee item Illinois rule
ISP petition to expunge/seal criminal history ISP fee schedule lists No Charge
ISP court order processing ISP fee schedule lists $60
Circuit clerk filing/processing fees Vary by county/circuit unless waived
Fee waiver Available by court order, including under Supreme Court Rule 298
If the same record was already sealed and later expunged Expungement petition fee for that same record is waived under 5.2(d)(10)
The ISP fee schedule lists no charge for a petition to expunge or seal criminal history and $60 for a court order to expunge or seal criminal history. (

"https://isp.illinois.gov/BureauOfIdentification/FeeSchedule?utm_source=chatgpt.com") Section 5.2 allows fees but waives required payment if the petitioner has a court fee-waiver order or another waiver; it also waives the fee for expunging the same record previously sealed. ("https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/002026300K5.2.htm")

sourceSections.title: Wilma intake questions for Illinois
sourceSections.text:
Ask in this order:
1
Was this an Illinois state case, federal case, or out-of-state case?

2
Were you under 18 when it happened, and was it handled in juvenile court or adult court?

3
What county, court, and case number?

4
Was there an arrest, ticket, or charge not initiated by arrest?

5
How did the case end: released without charging, dismissed, acquitted, no probable cause, supervision, qualified probation, conviction, reversed/vacated conviction, pardon, certificate of innocence, or still pending?

6
What was the exact charge and statute?

7
Was the case a DUI, reckless driving, minor traffic offense, domestic battery, protection-order/no-contact violation, sex offense, offense against a minor, animal cruelty, registry offense, or violent offense?

8
Did you successfully complete supervision or probation?

9
Was it qualified probation under a drug/cannabis/meth/second-chance/substance-use statute?

10
When did the last sentence, supervision, or qualified probation terminate?

11
Do you have any pending criminal charges?

12
Was the case cannabis-related? If yes, amount, statute, date, and violent-crime connection?

13
Was the case connected to trafficking, coercion, exploitation, survival circumstances, or felony prostitution?

14
Was someone else using your name or identity?

15
Do you have your ISP Access and Review RAP sheet, court dispositions, and Chicago RAP sheet if applicable?

Agent-facing summary
sourceSections.text:
For Illinois, train Wilma with this core logic:
Start with adult vs juvenile, then disposition.Non-convictions generally expunge immediately unless excluded.Supervision can expunge after 2 years, 5 years, or age 25 depending on offense.Qualified probation can expunge after 5 years if successfully completed.Most convictions route to sealing, not expungement.Clean Slate automatic sealing is not immediate; users still file petitions if they want relief sooner.Cannabis, trafficking, felony prostitution, identity theft, pardons, and certificates of innocence have special routes.Do not promise relief until Wilma has the ISP RAP sheet, court dispositions, exact statutes, final disposition, sentence/supervision completion date, pending-charge status, and county/court information.

===== SOURCE FILE: Illinois-Expungement-Sealing-Agent-Reference.pdf =====

Illinois Expungement & Sealing
Agent Training Reference
A complete operational knowledge base for record-clearing eligibility, the
expungement/sealing distinction, waiting periods, exclusions, forms, and filing under
Illinois law.

Coverage Statewide (all 102 counties; Circuit Court of each county; Cook County's 6 districts)

Primary statute Criminal Identification Act, 20 ILCS 2630/5.2

Related law Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act (410 ILCS 705); Clean Slate Act (P.A. 104-0459 / HB 1836); Certificate statu

Currency Reflects law as of mid-2026, including Clean Slate Act signed Jan 16, 2026

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING
Illinois law is mid-transition. The Clean Slate Act (HB 1836, P.A. 104-0459) was signed January 16,
2026. Most provisions take effect June 1 / June 30, 2026; the shortened sealing waiting period (3
to 2 years for certain records) and removal of the drug-test requirement take effect June 30, 2026;
and AUTOMATIC sealing does not begin until January 1, 2029, phasing in through 2034. Until
automatic sealing is live, the petition-based process remains the only route for most records. The
agent must not tell a user their record will be sealed automatically before 2029, and must verify
which effective date applies to any given rule.

How the agent should use this document
Illinois differs fundamentally from petition-by-offense-level states. The master question is not "misdemeanor or
felony" but expungement vs. sealing. Expungement physically destroys or returns the record and is generally
available only for non-convictions and supervision/qualified-probation dispositions. Sealing hides the record
from most public view (but not law enforcement) and is available for most misdemeanor and felony
convictions, subject to an exclusion list. The agent should always (1) determine the disposition type first, (2)
decide expungement vs. sealing from that, (3) check the offense against the exclusion list, then (4) apply the
waiting period.

CONTENTS

sourceSections.title: Sample petitions and completed examples
sourceSections.text:
Statutory shorthand used throughout

5.2(b) Expungement provisions (non-convictions, supervision, qualified probation)

5.2(c) Sealing provisions and the list of offenses that may NOT be sealed

5.2(a)(1)(G) "Minor traffic offense" definition — these don't affect eligibility

5.2(d) The filing/objection procedure (5 steps; 60-day objection window)

410 ILCS 705 Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act — minor-cannabis automatic expungement

730 ILCS 5/5-5.5 Certificates of Good Conduct / Relief From Disabilities / Eligibility for Sealing

P.A. 104-0459 Clean Slate Act (HB 1836) — automatic sealing, eff. phases 2026–2034

sourceSections.title: Eligibility rules: expungement vs. sealing
sourceSections.text:
The two remedies are not interchangeable, and a record is usually eligible for one or the other, not both. The
agent's first job is to route the user to the correct remedy.

Expungement (record destroyed or returned)
Available, generally, when the case did NOT end in a conviction:

• Arrests or charges that did not lead to a conviction (no charges filed, dismissed, nolle prosequi, stricken with
leave, or a finding of not guilty / acquittal).
• Cases that ended in court supervision — supervision is not a conviction, so most supervision dispositions are
expungeable after the waiting period.
• Qualified probation successfully completed and the case dismissed: 410 (drug), 710, 1410, TASC, Second
Chance probation.
• A conviction that was reversed, vacated, or for which the petitioner was found factually innocent.
• A conviction pardoned by the Governor with express authorization to expunge.
• Certain minor cannabis offenses (often handled automatically — see Section 9 and the cannabis note below).

Sealing (record hidden from most public view)
Available, generally, for convictions that cannot be expunged:

• Most misdemeanor convictions (except those on the exclusion list in Section 3).
• Most felony convictions — since 2017's HB 2373, nearly all felony classes are sealable, with limited
exceptions, after the waiting period. (Before 2017 only nine felonies qualified.)
• Convictions, first-offender probation, 410/TASC probation that resulted in a conviction.
• Sealed records remain visible to law enforcement, courts, and certain licensing bodies; they are hidden from
employers, landlords, and the general public.

Cannabis — a special automatic track
Under the Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act, a "minor cannabis offense" (possession of not more than 30
grams, no penalty enhancement, not tied to a violent crime) committed before June 25, 2019 is eligible for
automatic expungement of the arrest record by the Illinois State Police on a rolling timeline (records pre-2000
by Jan 1 2025; 2000–2012 by Jan 1 2023; 2013–6/25/2019 by Jan 1 2021). Minor-cannabis convictions route
through the Prisoner Review Board for a Governor's pardon authorizing expungement. Possession of 30–500
grams, or Class 4 PWID of 30 grams or less, requires a petition to expunge.

Illinois Expungement & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

AGENT GUIDANCE
Decision order every time: (1) Did it end in a conviction? If no, expungement. If yes, sealing unless
reversed/vacated/pardoned-to-expunge. (2) Is the offense on the sealing exclusion list? If yes, neither
petition route works and you should discuss certificates or wait for any future eligibility. (3) Apply the
waiting period.
Supervision is the most commonly misunderstood disposition: it is NOT a conviction and IS expungeable.
If a user says they "got supervision," route to expungement, not sealing.

Illinois Expungement & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Waiting periods
sourceSections.text:
Waiting periods run from the termination of the petitioner's last sentence (Illinois deems a sentence
terminated even with an outstanding financial obligation). Note the Clean Slate Act shortens several sealing
periods effective June 30, 2026.

Disposition / matter Remedy Waiting period Notes

No conviction (dismissed, Expunge None Eligible immediately
acquitted, no charges)

Reversed / vacated Expunge None Subject to narrow exclusions
conviction

Court supervision Expunge 2 years After completion of supervision
(most offenses)

Supervision: DUI-adjacent, Expunge 5 years Longer period by offense
domestic battery, crim.
sexual abuse

Qualified probation Expunge 5 years Clean Slate removes the
(410/710/1410/TASC/2nd Chance) 30-day drug-test rule (6/30/26)

Misdemeanor / ordinance Seal 3 yrs -> 2 yrs Clean Slate shortens to 2 yrs
conviction (eff. 6/30/2026)

Felony conviction Seal 3 years From termination of last
(eligible classes) felony sentence

Any eligible record + Seal 0 (waived) HS diploma/GED, associate's,
educational milestone bachelor's, career/voc cert

The educational waiver is a powerful and underused tool: if the petitioner completed a high school diploma,
GED, associate's, bachelor's, or a career/vocational/technical certificate during the period of their last sentence
(and had not already earned that same credential), the 3-year sealing wait can be waived entirely. The Clean
Slate Act preserves the educational waiver but, per Illinois Legal Aid, the 2-year period generally cannot be
waived.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Compute every waiting period from the end of the LAST sentence, not the offense or conviction date. A
later, unrelated sentence resets the clock.
Always screen for the educational waiver — ask whether the user earned any qualifying credential during
their sentence. It can take a 3-year wait to zero.
For sealing waiting periods, check the date: before 6/30/2026 use 3 years for misdemeanors; on or after,
use 2 years. State the effective-date dependency rather than guessing.

Illinois Expungement & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Disqualifying offenses (cannot be sealed)
sourceSections.text:
These offenses are excluded from sealing under 20 ILCS 2630/5.2(c), whether the disposition was court
supervision or a conviction. The Clean Slate Act did not add to or remove from this list — a record ineligible
before remains ineligible. If a matter is on this list and ended in a conviction, neither expungement nor sealing is
generally available.

• DUI and aggravated DUI (even when the disposition was supervision rather than conviction).
• Reckless driving — unless the petitioner was under 25 at the time of the offense and has no other
DUI/reckless-driving convictions (the youthful-offender exception).
• Domestic battery and aggravated domestic battery; felony domestic battery is also excluded.
• Violation of an order of protection.
• Sex offenses under Article 11 of the Criminal Code (with a narrow exception historically made for
prostitution), and any offense requiring sex-offender registration.
• Any sexual offense committed against a minor.
• Crimes of violence as defined under the Crime Victims Compensation Act and most serious violent felonies
(e.g., homicide, Class X felonies).
• Class A misdemeanors and above under the Humane Care for Animals Act (animal cruelty).
• Other misdemeanor offenses listed under Article 11 of the Criminal Code.

Things that do NOT disqualify
• Minor traffic offenses (petty/business offenses, Class C misdemeanors under the Vehicle Code) do not
affect eligibility to expunge or seal other records — but note they cannot themselves be expunged or sealed.
• Prior felony convictions. Under the Clean Slate framework you are no longer automatically disqualified
from sealing a felony merely because of another felony conviction.

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING
These are statutory categories, not labels. Map the user's actual charge to its Criminal Code
section before declaring it excluded or eligible. The domestic-battery and Article 11 boundaries in
particular were reshaped by P.A. 101-223 and P.A. 101-651, and the youthful-offender
reckless-driving exception turns on exact age and history. Verify against the current 5.2(c) text.

AGENT GUIDANCE
If a conviction is on this list, do not promise relief. Pivot to whether a non-conviction disposition exists,
whether a certificate (Relief From Disabilities / Good Conduct / Eligibility for Sealing) could help with
specific barriers, or whether any future statutory change applies.
DUI is the single most common disqualifier users misjudge — it is excluded even when the result was
supervision. Flag DUI early.

Illinois Expungement & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Case outcome rules
sourceSections.text:
Disposition type maps to remedy. This table is the agent's primary routing logic.

Case outcome Remedy Mandatory / discretionary Key condition

No charges filed / arrest only Expunge Court generally must grant Waiting period if any

Dismissed / nolle prosequi / Expunge Generally granted No conviction entered
stricken w/ leave

Found not guilty (acquittal) Expunge Generally granted No conviction entered

Court supervision (completed) Expunge Discretionary Waiting period (2 or 5 yr)

Qualified probation (410/710/ Expunge Discretionary 5-yr wait
1410/TASC/2nd Chance), dismissed

Conviction reversed / vacated / Expunge Mandatory on innocence finding Court order
factual innocence

Misdemeanor / felony conviction Seal Discretionary Waiting period; not excluded
(eligible class)

Excluded offense (Sec. 3) Neither Not available Consider certificates

After a court enters an expungement or sealing order, it provides copies to the Illinois State Police, the
petitioner, the State's Attorney, the arresting agency, the chief legal officer of the arresting unit of local
government, and any other ordered agencies. Agencies have up to 60 days from the order to process it. Once
sealed, Illinois law generally prohibits most employers from asking whether a person ever had a record
expunged or sealed.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Distinguish mandatory from discretionary in your framing. Non-conviction expungements are close to
automatic on a proper petition; supervision and conviction-sealing are discretionary and the judge weighs
the offense, history, and rehabilitation.
Expungement removes the record entirely; sealing only hides it from the public. Be precise — users often
say "expunge" when only sealing is available for a conviction.

Illinois Expungement & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Required forms
sourceSections.text:
Unlike Mississippi, Illinois HAS a mandatory statewide form suite. The Illinois Supreme Court Commission on
Access to Justice, with the Office of the State Appellate Defender (OSAD), publishes standardized
expungement/sealing forms that every Illinois circuit court is required to accept. The agent should always
steer users to these official forms rather than drafting a bespoke pleading.

The core statewide forms
1 Request to Expunge and Impound and/or Seal Criminal Records — the main petition; one form covers
both remedies via checkboxes, listing each arrest/case to be cleared.

2 Notice of Filing — served on the State's Attorney, arresting agency, chief legal officer, and Illinois State
Police to notify them of the request and start their objection window.
3 Order to Expunge and Impound and/or Seal Criminal Records — the proposed order the judge signs;
arrest information must be completed and submitted with the Request.

4 Fee Waiver Application (Application for Waiver of Court Fees) — for petitioners who cannot afford the
filing and State Police fees.

Where to get them
• Illinois Courts Standardized State Forms website (illinoiscourts.gov) — official fillable PDFs.
• Office of the State Appellate Defender (osad.illinois.gov) — forms plus eligibility worksheets; OSAD
Expungement Unit help line.
• Illinois Legal Aid Online (illinoislegalaid.org) — a free guided "Easy Form" interview that assembles the
completed forms (it does not e-file them).

Supporting material
• The petitioner's statewide criminal-history transcript (RAP sheet) from the Illinois State Police (see Section
9).
• Case/disposition information for every arrest and charge being cleared (dates, arresting agency, case number,
disposition).
• For qualified-probation expungements filed before 6/30/2026, historically a clean drug test within 30 days —
the Clean Slate Act removes this requirement effective 6/30/2026.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Because the forms are mandatory statewide, never tell a user a county requires a different bespoke
petition. The Request, Notice, and Order travel together; an Order with arrest info must accompany the
Request at filing.
Recommend the Illinois Legal Aid Easy Form to users who want guided drafting, but remind them it does
not file for them — they still file with the circuit clerk and (often) e-file.

Illinois Expungement & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
The statewide 5-step process under 20 ILCS 2630/5.2(d):

1 Get your criminal-history transcript (RAP sheet) from the Illinois State Police so every arrest and
disposition is accounted for.

2 Complete the Request, Notice, and Order. Mark expunge vs. seal per case; list each arrest with dates,
arresting agency, case number, and disposition.

3 File with the circuit clerk in the county where the arrest or charge occurred — venue is the
sentencing/charging county, not the county of residence. Pay the filing fee (or file a Fee Waiver).

4 Serve notice on the State's Attorney, the arresting agency, the chief legal officer of the arresting unit of local
government, and the Illinois State Police, using the Notice of Filing.
5 Objection window and hearing. Those agencies have 60 days to object. If no objection, many courts grant
without a hearing; if there is an objection, the court sets a hearing and the petitioner presents their case. The
judge enters the Order; agencies then have up to 60 days to process it.

Multiple cases and counties
• File in each county where you have records; a separate filing is generally needed per county.
• In Cook County, only one fee is charged for all petitions filed on the same date, even across multiple
districts; Cook County has 6 districts and you file in the district matching the case.
• If a Cook County order is not processed within ~70 days, the petitioner can mail a copy of the order plus
proof of payment (or fee waiver) to the Illinois State Police Expungement Unit to push it through.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Pin down the county (and Cook County district) of each arrest before advising on filing — venue is
non-negotiable and per-county.
Set expectations on timing: the typical Illinois expungement/sealing runs about 4–6 months from filing to
final order, longer if an agency objects, plus up to 60 days for agencies to process.

Illinois Expungement & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Court-specific quirks
sourceSections.text:
• Standardized forms, local filing rules. The forms are uniform statewide, but copy counts, e-filing rules, and
mailing procedures differ by county — petitioners should call the clerk first.
• Cook County single-fee rule. One fee covers all same-day petitions across districts; this is a meaningful
saving for users with multiple cases. Cook also has dedicated expungement divisions.
• Specialized judges. Larger counties route these to judges in special expungement/sealing divisions who may
rotate; in smaller counties the original sentencing judge often hears the petition.
• Cook County processing delays. Documented backlogs; the ISP-Expungement-Unit mail-in remedy (after
~70 days) is a known workaround.
• State Police processing fee. Most counties bundle an ISP fee with the clerk fee (e.g., McLean County's $136
total = $60 filing + $60 ISP + $16 for four certified copies). Amounts vary by county.
• Cannabis is mostly automatic. Many minor-cannabis arrest records are cleared by ISP without any filing;
advise users to verify via the ISP Access and Review process before paying to petition.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Treat the forms as fixed but the filing logistics as county-specific. Default advice: call the circuit clerk for
copy counts, fee amount, and e-filing rules before filing.
For Chicago/Cook users with several cases, lead with the single-fee-per-day rule — it changes the filing
strategy.

Illinois Expungement & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Fee information
sourceSections.text:
Item Typical amount Note

Total filing cost (per Request) ~$120–$160 Varies by county; bundles clerk + ISP fees

Circuit clerk filing fee ~$60 Set by county

Illinois State Police processing fee ~$60 Statewide ISP charge per request

Certified copies of the Order ~$16 for four Sent to agencies on completion
(then ~$4/set)

Fee waiver $0 Application for Waiver of Court Fees if unable to pay

Cook County (same-day petitions) One fee total Covers multiple petitions filed same date

Cannabis automatic expungement $0 Handled by ISP; no petition fee

Most counties roll the clerk fee, the Illinois State Police fee, and the cost of certified copies into one payment
(McLean County's published $136 is a representative example). The fee is generally charged per Request filed,
with the Cook County same-day exception. Any petitioner who cannot afford the cost should file the fee-waiver
application rather than delay.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Quote a range ($120–$160 typical) rather than a fixed number, and point the user to the specific clerk for
the exact amount. Always surface the fee waiver for cost-constrained users.
Cannabis automatic expungement is free — do not let a user pay to petition for something ISP may
already be clearing automatically.

Illinois Expungement & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Criminal record (RAP sheet) request process
sourceSections.text:
An accurate statewide criminal-history transcript is step one of any Illinois filing — the petition must list every
arrest and disposition, and the user usually cannot reconstruct that from memory.

sourceSections.title: Sample petitions and completed examples
sourceSections.text:
Illinois uses a single combined statewide Request form with checkboxes for expunge vs. seal. The examples
below show how that form is completed for each of the three most common scenarios. Names, dates, and
identifiers are fictional and illustrative.

Example A — Expungement of a non-conviction (charge dismissed)
IN THE CIRCUIT COURT OF COOK COUNTY, ILLINOIS
REQUEST TO EXPUNGE AND IMPOUND AND/OR SEAL CRIMINAL RECORDS
Petitioner: Andre L. Carter DOB: 07/22/1992
[X] EXPUNGE [ ] SEAL
Arrest 1:
Arresting agency: Chicago Police Department
Arrest date: 05/03/2021 Case/IR No.: 21-CR-118842
Offense charged: Retail theft (Class A misdemeanor)
Disposition: Nolle prosequi (dismissed) on 11/09/2021
Basis: No conviction was entered; record is eligible for
expungement under 20 ILCS 2630/5.2(b). No waiting period.
Relief requested: expunge all records of this arrest and charge;
order ISP and the arresting agency to destroy/return records.
Notice served on: State's Attorney, Chicago PD, chief legal
officer, and Illinois State Police (Notice of Filing attached).

Example B — Expungement after court supervision
IN THE CIRCUIT COURT OF DUPAGE COUNTY, ILLINOIS
REQUEST TO EXPUNGE ... [X] EXPUNGE [ ] SEAL
Petitioner: Maria S. Delgado DOB: 02/14/1995
Arrest 1:
Arresting agency: DuPage County Sheriff
Arrest date: 03/18/2022 Case No.: 2022-CM-000455
Offense: Possession of drug paraphernalia
Disposition: Court supervision; completed 03/18/2023
Basis: Supervision is not a conviction and is expungeable
2 years after successful completion (20 ILCS 2630/5.2(b)).
Supervision completed 03/18/2023; eligible 03/18/2025.
Educational waiver: Petitioner earned an associate's degree
during the supervision term -- waiting period waived.
Relief requested: expunge all records of this arrest and
supervision disposition.

Example C — Sealing of an eligible felony conviction
IN THE CIRCUIT COURT OF WILL COUNTY, ILLINOIS
REQUEST TO ... [ ] EXPUNGE [X] SEAL
Petitioner: Darnell R. Pierce DOB: 11/30/1988
Arrest 1:
Arresting agency: Joliet Police Department
Arrest date: 06/12/2019 Case No.: 2019-CF-001203
Offense: Possession of a controlled substance
(Class 4 felony) -- NOT an excluded offense
Disposition: Convicted; sentence completed 08/01/2022
Basis: Eligible felony conviction may be SEALED 3 years after

Illinois Expungement & Sealing — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

termination of the last sentence (20 ILCS 2630/5.2(c)).
Sentence terminated 08/01/2022; eligible 08/01/2025.
(No drug-test attachment required as of 6/30/2026.)
Confirmation: offense is not DUI, domestic battery, a sex
offense, animal cruelty, or a violent/Class X felony.
Relief requested: seal all records of this conviction from
public access; record remains available to law enforcement.

Notice of Filing and Order (all types)
Every Request is accompanied by a Notice of Filing served on the State's Attorney, arresting agency, chief
legal officer, and Illinois State Police (opening the 60-day objection window), and a proposed Order to
Expunge and Impound and/or Seal with the arrest information completed for the judge's signature. On entry,
the clerk distributes certified copies to ISP and the other agencies, which have up to 60 days to process.

This reference is an internal training and retrieval aid for the Wilma assistant. It summarizes Illinois statutes and common practice
2630/5.2 text, the applicable Clean Slate effective dates, ISP record-request procedures, and county filing specifics before relying
on any rule here.
